questions.q: What is the main objective of accounting for not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: The main objective of accounting for not-for-profit organisations is to ascertain the financial position and performance of the organisation.

questions.q: What is the difference between cash and accrual basis of accounting?
questions.answer: On cash basis of accounting, revenues and expenses are recorded when cash is received or paid, whereas on accrual basis, revenues and expenses are recorded when they are earned or incurred, respectively.

questions.q: What is meant by 'Fund' in the context of not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: A fund is a specific purpose or activity for which money is collected and is to be spent. It is a separate and distinct entity within the organisation.

questions.q: What is the purpose of preparing a statement of financial performance for a not-for-profit organisation?
questions.answer: The purpose of preparing a statement of financial performance is to present the financial performance of the organisation in a specific period, highlighting its income and expenses.

questions.q: How is an unrestricted fund different from a restricted fund?
questions.answer: An unrestricted fund is a fund for which there are no restrictions on its use, whereas a restricted fund is a fund for which the use is limited or restricted by a donor or other external agency.

questions.q: What is the significance of an asset's net realisable value (NRV) in accounting for not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: The NRV of an asset represents its current market value and is used to determine the amount of loss or gain on its disposal. It is also used to value inventory and other assets.

questions.q: What is the purpose of preparing a balance sheet for a not-for-profit organisation?
questions.answer: The purpose of preparing a balance sheet is to present the financial position of the organisation at a specific point in time, highlighting its assets, liabilities, and equity.

questions.q: How is a donor-imposed restriction different from a self-imposed restriction?
questions.answer: A donor-imposed restriction is a restriction imposed by a donor on the use of a fund, whereas a self-imposed restriction is a restriction imposed by the organisation itself.

questions.q: What is the significance of accruals and prepayments in accounting for not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: Accruals and prepayments are used to match revenues and expenses with the period in which they are earned or incurred, ensuring that the financial performance of the organisation is accurately presented.

questions.q: What is the difference between a permanent and a temporary difference?
questions.answer: A permanent difference is a difference that is expected to continue indefinitely, whereas a temporary difference is a difference that is expected to reverse in the future.

questions.q: How are deferred revenue expenses accounted for in not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: Deferred revenue expenses are accounted for by debiting the revenue expense and crediting the deferred revenue expense account, and then amortising the deferred revenue expense over the period in which the related revenue is earned.

questions.q: What is the purpose of preparing a statement of cash flows for a not-for-profit organisation?
questions.answer: The purpose of preparing a statement of cash flows is to present the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents of the organisation during a specific period, highlighting its liquidity and cash management.

questions.q: How are cash and cash equivalents defined in the context of not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: Cash includes cash on hand, in banks, and other places. Cash equivalents are highly liquid, short-term investments that can be easily converted into cash.

questions.q: What is the significance of accounting for contingencies in not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: Accounting for contingencies is significant because it requires the disclosure of potential losses or gains that may arise from future events, ensuring that the financial position and performance of the organisation are accurately presented.
key_points: Accurately present financial position and performance

questions.q: How are contingencies accounted for in not-for-profit organisations?
questions.answer: Contingencies are accounted for by disclosing their nature and potential impact on the financial position and performance of the organisation, and by making a provision for the expected loss if a loss is probable and estimable.

questions.q: What is the purpose of preparing a statement of changes in fund balances or net assets for a not-for-profit organisation?
questions.answer: The purpose of preparing a statement of changes in fund balances or net assets is to present the changes in the fund balances or net assets of the organisation during a specific period, highlighting its financial performance and changes in net assets.
key_points: Changes in fund balances or net assets
Financial performance and changes in net assets
questions.topic: Statement of Changes in Fund Balances or Net Assets
